Cervicogenic Headaches
Cervicogenic headaches are a constellation of symptoms that rep-
resent the common referral patterns of cervical spine structures. 
Various spine structures have been implicated in cervicogenic 
headache, including nerve roots and spinal nerves, dorsal root 
ganglia, uncovertebral joints, intervertebral discs, facet joints, 
ligaments, and muscles.
219 Cervicogenic headaches have been 
conceptualized as being primarily unilateral and stemming from 
the upper cervical spine. Symptoms can spread to involve the 
contralateral side,
222 but typically the side of the initial referral 
source remains most intense.218
Epidemiology and Pathophysiology
The prevalence of cervicogenic headache has been reported to 
range from 0.4% to 2.5% in the general population
217 to as high 
as 36.2% in patients with a complaint of headaches. 9,169 Females 
are more commonly affected (79.1%) than males (20.9%), 250 
with a mean age of 42.9 years and an average symptom duration 
of 6.8 years.
82 Various spine structures have been implicated in 
cervicogenic headache, including nerve roots and spinal nerves, 
dorsal root ganglia, uncovertebral joints, intervertebral discs, facet 
joints, ligaments, and muscles.
219 Circumstantial evidence sup-
ports the convergence theory to explain why cranial symptoms 
can occur due to cervical spine pain generators. Convergence of 
two separate primary afferents derived from two different body 
regions on the same second-order intraspinal neurons allows the 
nociceptive activity of one afferent nerve to be perceived as p ain 
in the distribution of the other afferent nerve.
23 The cervicogenic 
headache can be due to degenerative changes, a direct result of 
trauma, or occur without any underlying biomechanical insult to 
the various cervical spine structures subserved by cervical afferent 
fibers. The C2-C3 zygapophyseal joint
47,142,143 and the C2-C3, 
C3-C4, C4-C5, and C5-C6 intervertebral discs 228 have been  
primarily implicated as sources of cervicogenic headache.
Diagnosis
History and Physical Examination.  Support for a structural 
source within the cervical spine as the etiology of the patient’s 
headaches is obtained by eliciting any history of prior head or 
neck trauma, such as a whiplash event.
218 Whiplash events, such 
as motor vehicle accidents, have been associated with injury to the 
cervical zygapophyseal joints, intervertebral discs, or nerve roots, 
either in isolation or in combination.
16,142,242 The character of the 
pain can vary from a deep ache to sharp and stabbing. The dura -
tion of the painful symptoms fluctuates from initial episodic 
bouts of pain, progressing to more chronic and constant pain. 
Patients often describe the pain as initiating in the cervical region 
and traveling to the head and the neck as the pain becomes severe. 
The cervicogenic headache can then become the primary com-
plaint, overshadowing the original cervical axial pain.
The duration of symptoms ranges from a few hours to a few 
weeks but characteristically lasts longer than that associated with

have also been described but are not common.
70
Physical examination of the patient with complaints of cervi -
cogenic headache typically reveals reduced active ROM due to 
muscle guarding, arthritic changes, or soft tissue inflexibility. If 
the cervicogenic headache is being produced by a cervical zyg-
apophyseal joint, the patient can usually pinpoint with one finger 
or with the palm of the hand a unilateral area of maximal pain. 
Cervical intervertebral disc–induced cervicogenic headache typi-
cally begins as midline pain that spreads across the spine and into 
the head or face. However, unilateral occipital headache symp -
toms that are greater than the neck pain following a traumatic 
event are more suggestive of zygapophyseal joint pain than disco-
genic symptoms.
16,142 Certain head and neck movements can 
precipitate painful symptoms, such as axial rotation or cervical 
extension. We have commonly seen patients report an onset after 
sleeping in an awkward position. Spurling maneuver does not 
reproduce upper limb radicular symptoms but usually aggravates 
the axial pain, and patients report reproduction of their paramid-
line zygapophyseal joint–mediated pain. This pain can also often 
be reproduced with deep palpation over the involved joint. Apply-
ing the diagnostic criteria from the International Headache  
Society, a cervicogenic headache includes any headache that is (A) 
accompanied by clinical and/or imaging evidence of a disorder or 
lesion within the cervical spine or soft tissues of the neck known 
to cause headache
2; (B) accompanied by evidence of causation 
demonstrated by at least two of the following: (1) headache has 
developed in temporal relation to the onset of the cervical disor -
der or appearance of the lesion, (2) headache has significantly 
improved or resolved in parallel with improvement in or resolu -
tion of the cervical disorder or lesion, (3) cervical ROM is reduced 
and headache is made significantly worse by provocative maneu -
vers, or (4) headache is abolished following diagnostic blockade  
of a cervical structure or its nerve supply; and (C) not better  
accounted for by another International Classification of Headache 
Disorders (Third Edition) diagnosis.
87
Imaging Studies. A history of trauma requires cervical flexion 
and extension lateral radiographs to detect abnormal segmental 
motion. It also requires anteroposterior views, including an open 
mouth view of the odontoid process, to rule out fractures  
(Fig. 33.27).
164 Any suspicion for fracture mandates a subsequent 
cervical CT scan performed with multiplanar reformatted images 
to better delineate the osseous injury.
164 MRI is better than CT at 
evaluating the intervertebral discs for desiccation, decreased disc 
height, and frank herniation. However, MRI has a false-positive 
rate of 51% and a false-negative rate of 27% in detecting painful 
discs identified by discography.
266 Abnormalities seen on imaging 
studies should be clinically correlated because such findings can 
occur lifelong in asymptomatic individuals.
21,155,243
Functional Diagnostic Tests and Treatment. Physical therapy 
is the first step of treatment for cervicogenic headaches. Manipu -
lative therapy, gentle stretching, and manual cervical traction 
should be incorporated into this initial treatment regimen as well. 
If more conservative measures do not provide complete relief, 
interventional procedures can be considered.
4 As mentioned, cer-
vicogenic headaches due to upper cervical zygapophyseal joint 
pain can be studied with confirmatory diagnostic blockade. The 
traditional algorithm includes diagnostic blocks performed  
sequentially at the C3-C4 and C1-C2 joints after assessing the 
C2-C3 joint by third occipital nerve blockade.
16 Following a  
zygapophyseal joint mapping study, we now also incorporate 
blockade of the C4-C5 zygapophyseal joints if the headaches  
include anterior head or facial symptoms. Once the painful joint 
is identified, therapeutic procedures are performed as outlined 
under treatment of cervical zygapophyseal joint pain for C1-C2 
but medial branch neurotomy (or third occipital nerve neurot-
omy for the C2-C3 joint) for other levels.
Cervical ESIs have been helpful in treating cervicogenic  
headaches. These injections are effective because they reduce the 
inflammation around cervical nerve roots, providing the patient 
with pain relief.
4
Whiplash Syndrome
Whiplash (acceleration-deceleration injury) should be conceptual-
ized as having three components. The whiplash event is the biome-
chanical effect incurred by the occupants of one vehicle when 
struck by another vehicle. The whiplash injury is the impairment, or 
injured structure, resulting from the whiplash event. The whiplash 
syndrome is the set of symptoms arising from the whiplash injury.
13 
Cervical whiplash occurs when an acceleration-deceleration force 
injures the cervical spine and its supporting structures. During a 
whiplash event, the head and neck do not suffer a direct blow, but 
each undergoes an excursion due to the inertial response of the 
body to the forces imparted on it.
13 Rear-end collisions represent 
the most common pattern of whiplash-related injury, but injury 
due to head-on and side collisions can also occur.
13

Regardless of the direction of impact, whiplash is defined by the 
passive movement of the neck. Muscular control to stabilize the 
cervical spine does not react quickly enough to prevent injurious 
forces from occurring across the cervical functional spinal units. 
Both the lack of muscular pillaring and the generation of abnormal 
forces resulting in movements around abnormal axes of rotation 
subject the passive restraining structures to abnormal strain. 13,116 
For example, at 110 ms after rear-end impact, the C5-C6 disc 
space widens anteriorly, causing abutment of the articular pro -
cesses of the C5-C6 zygapophyseal joint 116 and posterior shear 
within the disc. 174 The anterior disc, anterior longitudinal liga -
ment, posterior disc or annulus, and cervical zygapophyseal joints 
are all at risk of injury during a whiplash event.13,116,174 Injury also 
occurs to the cervical soft tissues, resulting in strain and sprain 
injuries.168 These injuries typically heal over a relatively short time 
period, as would be expected for soft tissue injury. The most com-
monly reported symptoms of whiplash injury include neck pain 
and headaches, followed by shoulder girdle pain, upper limb par-
esthesias, and weakness. Less common symptoms include dizzi -
ness, visual disturbances, and tinnitus.13
Most patients suffering from whiplash syndrome are destined 
to recover within the first 2 to 3 months after the injury, and after 
2 years 82% are symptom free. 186 Severely afflicted patients ac -
count for only 6% of all whiplash patients at 3 months, and this 
percentage decreases minimally to 4% at 2 years. 186 Studies have 
demonstrated that chronicity of whiplash symptoms develops 
independent of litigation,170 and litigation does not alter response 
to treatment.202 The clinician should pursue evaluation and treat-
ment of whiplash-related symptoms rather than just assume that 
the pain is due to mitigating circumstances, such as secondary 
gain considerations.
T reatment guidelines for whiplash typically follow a conserva-
tive, stepwise approach. For acute cases, recommended treatments 
include activity modification, cryotherapy, home exercises, and 
physical therapy, along with conservative medications such as 
NSAIDs and acetaminophen. Opioids are discouraged due to 
increased risk of long-term use and lack of specificity. 212 Early 
high-intensity care has been linked to slower recovery, while edu-
cation and reassurance are advised during the acute phase. 220 
Chronic whiplash may require evaluating psychosocial and  
organic factors,144,182 with antidepressants or anticonvulsants for 
neuropathic pain.49 Manual therapies such as manipulation and 
mobilization are most effective when combined with exercise, 
though risks, including arterial dissection, exist. 56,77,83,97,105,213,215 
Careful technique can reduce these risks, especially avoiding  
extension-based, high-velocity, low-amplitude thrusts. Interven -
tional procedures, such as radiofrequency ablation, can effectively 
manage cervical facet pain, offering months of relief. 151
Summary
Neck pain is one of the most common complaints of patients 
seeking medical attention. Knowledge of spinal biomechanics 
and pathophysiology helps determine the most likely pain gen -
erators in each case. A variety of spinal structures can produce 
overlapping or obscure symptomatology. An accurate diagnosis 
provides the best opportunity for effective treatment. The build -
ing blocks for successful therapeutic interventions include con -
trolling pain and inflammation while at the same time educating 
the patient about the injury, the treatment objectives, and the 
prognosis. As technology advances and our knowledge of cervical 
spine disorders grows, our clinical pathways for treatment of 
cervical problems will continue to evolve and improve. It is im -
portant to view the patient as a whole and to institute physical, 
pharmacologic, behavioral, and interventional treatments in the 
broad context of achieving what is best for the patient’s physio -
logic and psychological well-being.
